 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.1","title":"Phase 2: IoCap + IoOp Obligations","description":"# Phase 2: IoCap + IoOp Obligations

## Purpose
Integrate I/O into the structured concurrency + obligation model:
- all in-flight I/O operations are obligations bound to a region
- region close waits for I/O obligations (or escalates by policy)
- cancellation propagates to I/O ops and is driven to terminal states

## Core Concept
I/O must be modeled as a two-phase effect:
- submit/reserve (cancel-safe)
- complete/commit or cancel/abort

This prevents “invisible in-flight I/O” from violating quiescence.

## Acceptance Criteria
- I/O operations create `ObligationKind::IoOp` obligations.
- Region close cannot complete while I/O obligations are reserved.
- Cancellation requests attempt I/O cancellation and/or escalation with budgets.

 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.1.1","title":"Define IoCap and Cx::io surface","description":"# IoCap + Cx::io Surface

## Purpose
Introduce the I/O capability in the explicit capability boundary:
- production and lab runtimes implement the same `Cx`-level I/O surface
- I/O operations are impossible without `IoCap`

## API Sketch
```rust
pub trait IoCx {
    fn io(&mut self) -> &mut dyn IoCap;
}

pub trait IoCap {
    fn read(&mut self, file: FileHandle, buf: Buf) -> IoSubmit;
    // etc
}
```

We may choose async methods depending on how submission/completion is modeled.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `Cx` exposes I/O only when configured with IoCap.
- Tests can run with a lab IoCap.

 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.1.2","title":"Implement IoOp obligation lifecycle (submit/complete/cancel)","description":"# IoOp Obligation Lifecycle

## Purpose
Model every in-flight I/O operation as an obligation:
- created/reserved on submission
- resolved as committed on completion
- resolved as aborted on cancellation (if possible) or on policy escalation

## Semantics
- `submit` creates `ObligationKind::IoOp` in `Reserved` state.
- `complete` transitions to `Committed` and returns result.
- `cancel` attempts to abort; if kernel cannot cancel, policy decides whether to wait or escalate.

## Region Close Interaction
- Region close must not become `Closed(_)` while any `IoOp` remains `Reserved`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- IoOp obligations are registered in the registry.
- Trace captures submit/complete/cancel.

-{"id":"asupersync-ds8.2","title":"Phase 2: Reactor Abstraction + Deterministic Lab I/O","description":"# Phase 2: Reactor Abstraction + Deterministic Lab I/O

## Purpose
Provide a pluggable I/O backend:
- production: OS-backed reactor
- lab: deterministic virtual I/O for reproducible tests

## Requirements
- The I/O interface must be capability-gated (`IoCap`).
- Lab backend must be:
  - deterministic
  - replayable
  - able to simulate failures and delays

## Acceptance Criteria
- There is a `Reactor` trait (or equivalent) that the runtime calls.
- Lab runtime can run I/O-heavy workloads deterministically.

 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.2.1","title":"Define Reactor trait and runtime integration","description":"# Reactor Trait + Runtime Integration

## Purpose
Abstract over I/O backends so the runtime can:
- submit I/O
- receive completions
- drive tasks waiting on I/O

## Plan-of-Record
- A `Reactor` trait implemented by:
  - lab reactor (deterministic)
  - production backend(s)

- The scheduler must treat I/O completions as wake events.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Runtime can be compiled/run with a dummy reactor.
- Lab runtime uses lab reactor.

 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.2.2","title":"Implement deterministic lab reactor (virtual I/O + fault injection)","description":"# Deterministic Lab Reactor

## Purpose
Implement virtual I/O operations in lab mode:
- deterministic completion ordering
- deterministic timing (virtual time)
- configurable failure injection

## Features
- Simulate:
  - delays
  - partial reads/writes
  - interruptions
  - connection resets
- Drive completions through the scheduler as wake events.

## Acceptance Criteria
- I/O-heavy tests are reproducible.
- Trace includes I/O submit/complete events.

 {"id":"asupersync-ds8.3","title":"Phase 2: Platform I/O Backends (io_uring/kqueue/IOCP)","description":"# Phase 2: Platform I/O Backends (io_uring/kqueue/IOCP)

## Purpose
Implement production reactor backends per platform.

## Notes
This is large and may be staged:
- start with a single platform backend (likely Linux io_uring) once the core semantics are solid
- keep API stable and test using lab I/O first

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least one real backend exists and passes integration tests.
- Cancellation behavior matches the obligation/cancellation protocol.
